tweets.text:
Worth a listen with Bending Spoons filing to go public today. 

We talk about their origins, playbook, talent and everything in between.

Couple things I liked from their S-1:
- Luck plays a big role in finding PMF

- Luck is irrelevant when pursuing operational excellence

- Talent > experience: "Nearly all of our businesses and functions are led by people in their twenties or thirties, most of whom had little or no work experience prior to joining Bending Spoons."
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Luca Ferrari is the co-founder and CEO of @bendingspoons, one of the most fascinating companies in Europe and just became Italy's first decacorn.

He describes it as 25% private equity firm and 75% technology company. They fully acquire and rebuild digital companies like https://t.co/2ekWWqj1Ww

tweets.text:
Leopold Aschbrenner's last few years have been absolutely incredible. One day they'll probably make a movie out of this run. The fact that 20% of Situational Awareness is invested in Anthropic where his fiance is chief of staff is also just great entertainment/fodder for the storyline.

I also am intrigued by the setup looking like Anthropic is going to be the last of "the big 3 IPOs". With everyone staring down the onslaught of equity+debt issuance linked to the AI trade, you have to question whether the IPO window is going to stay open for all 3. Are investors going to be full by the time Anthropic comes to market?

I think 2H 2026 is going to be a memorable time in market history.

tweets.text:
The hyperscalers are trapped in a prisoner's dilemma.

Path A: Keep spending on AI capex → declining free cash flow, fewer buybacks, stocks keeps lagging.

Path B: Cut spending → balance sheets improve but it kills the semiconductor supply chain trade that's been carrying the Nasdaq and many of their investments.

The path where everyone wins is narrowing. The generals are no longer leading as the Mag7 relative performance vs Nasdaq divergence has never been this large. That's historically a warning sign.

thread_tweets.text: @Evan_ss6 It’s not just about the buyback flows but the why. Changing business and operating models lead to different run-rate financial metrics. Higher capex burdens, lower FCF yields and higher leverage profiles are all associated with more volatile and lower multiple industries.
thread_tweets.full_text: @Evan_ss6 It’s not just about the buyback flows but the why. Changing business and operating models lead to different run-rate financial metrics. Higher capex burdens, lower FCF yields and higher leverage profiles are all associated with more volatile and lower multiple industries.

thread_tweets.text:
That’s largely what my point is.
1) I don’t see a world going forward where capex roic expands from here. The competition is too great and there’s not enough monetization evidence from the hyperscalers for me to assume otherwise.
2) separate but related, still increasing capex due to non-financial factors (strategic imperative, first to AGI, etc) provides no comfort that it’s a lot of growth capex / little maintenance. Said another way, to stay relevant, I’m not sure how much can be curtailed, and that is despite the roic on each incremental dollar almost certainly falling.
3) if I’m wrong and they are able to curtail a large amount and still maintain relevance and top line + earnings/FCF growth, then that hurts the semis/beneficiary trade

The hyperscaler actors will eventually right size the situation and I think that will ultimately look like slowing capex due to less than ideal roic. I am more concerned about the downstream/receiver part of the trade wrt this topic. The hyperscalers have a separate but related liquidity/supply of securities/ipo issuance problem.

tweets.text:
Why I'm optimistic about perps

A perpetual is a leveraged bet on price with no expiry. Long or short; pay or collect funding to hold; close whenever. The simplest leverage tool in crypto, and the most used by far. Offshore perp volume topped ~$93T last year.

Why I think it keeps winning:

1. It already won once. BitMEX brought perps to crypto in 2016. Within two years, they overtook dated futures and options. BTC perp volume now runs ~6x spot.

2. First US approvals just landed. Kalshi's BTCPERP went live June 3: the first US-regulated perp. The CFTC cleared Coinbase to route clients into global perps. Kraken's lined up next.

3. The demand is structural. Retail was over half of US options volume last year. Global CFD volume runs past $30T a month. 4B+ people are still unbrokered. That's who perps are built for.

4. Simpler than options. No expiry, no theta decay, no implied vol to track. Exposure is linear, basically spot with leverage. For trades held days, not months, that's the whole appeal.

5. Cleaner than CFDs. CFDs do the same job, but OTC: opaque pricing, broker-set spreads, scattered liquidity. Perps run it on a transparent order book with no bilateral trust.

6. They trade 24/7. Markets move overnight and on weekends—on earnings and headlines, exactly when retail is shut out. Perps don't close.

7. Room to grow is huge. Options, futures, and CFDs do $8T+ a day. Perp DEXs do ~$20B. Catching even 1% is a different-sized business.

Perps started as a crypto workaround and became the better tool for leveraged directional trading. The US cracking the door just widens the funnel.

2. First US approvals just landed. Kalshi's BTCPERP went live June 3: the first US-regulated perp. The CFTC cleared Coinbase to route clients into global perps. Kraken's lined up next.

3. The demand is structural. Retail was over half of US options volume last year. Global CFD volume runs past $30T a month. 4B+ people are still unbrokered. That's who perps are built for.

4. Simpler than options. No expiry, no theta decay, no implied vol to track. Exposure is linear, basically spot with leverage. For trades held days, not months, that's the whole appeal.

5. Cleaner than CFDs. CFDs do the same job, but OTC: opaque pricing, broker-set spreads, scattered liquidity. Perps run it on a transparent order book with no bilateral trust.

6. They trade 24/7. Markets move overnight and on weekends—on earnings and headlines, exactly when retail is shut out. Perps don't close.

tweets.text: The same investor will react differently to the same idea based on how hot a deal you are and how much they like your personality. If you're not a hot deal and they don't like you, they'll look for reasons to hate the idea.

thread_tweets.text: The same investor will react differently to the same idea based on how hot a deal you are and how much they like your personality. If you're not a hot deal and they don't like you, they'll look for reasons to hate the idea.
thread_tweets.full_text: The same investor will react differently to the same idea based on how hot a deal you are and how much they like your personality. If you're not a hot deal and they don't like you, they'll look for reasons to hate the idea.

thread_tweets.text: They're not just pretending to dislike the idea in order to find a legitimate-sounding excuse for rejecting you. How hot a deal you are and how much they like your personality really does affect their opinion of the idea.
thread_tweets.full_text: They're not just pretending to dislike the idea in order to find a legitimate-sounding excuse for rejecting you. How hot a deal you are and how much they like your personality really does affect their opinion of the idea.

thread_tweets.text: This is one reason among many that you shouldn't take investors' opinions about your idea to heart. You shouldn't automatically ignore such opinions either. They might be right. But treat them the way you'd treat a fortune cookie.
thread_tweets.full_text: This is one reason among many that you shouldn't take investors' opinions about your idea to heart. You shouldn't automatically ignore such opinions either. They might be right. But treat them the way you'd treat a fortune cookie.

thread_tweets.text: @AlexJFRSalsali Not pretty privilege. What impresses investors is your manner, not how physically attractive you are. And indeed that does have a good deal of predictive value.
thread_tweets.full_text: @AlexJFRSalsali Not pretty privilege. What impresses investors is your manner, not how physically attractive you are. And indeed that does have a good deal of predictive value.

thread_tweets.text: @erikhabsburg A hot deal means a startup that lots of other investors are eager to invest in.
thread_tweets.full_text: @erikhabsburg A hot deal means a startup that lots of other investors are eager to invest in.

thread_tweets.text: I'm not saying investors are wrong to care about your personality and how hot a deal you are. The former has a huge effect on the outcome and even the latter has some signal in it. Their mistake is to let these factors leak into their judgment of the idea.

tweets.text:
$DEUS is currently trading at a $17M mcap, $34M FDV. If you deduct the 30% supply being held and governed by the DAO, it's just a $24M FDV.

So the fairest metric would be to say that XMAQUINA is trading at a 2.4x to NAV.

In comparison, RoboStrategy / $BOT is trading at a 5.1x to NAV with a strong uptrend and narrative forming around it.

There is an interesting catch up trade here in my opinion, on top of some additional tailwinds:

1) I still think robotics is not a consensus trade right now, but the "ChatGPT moment" of physical AI is yet to come. There will be explosive demand for these very inaccessible companies, and $DEUS and $BOT are currently the only vehicles out there that allow you to get exposure to them.

2) What's even more interesting is that XMAQUINA will soon evolve into a Robotics Capital Markets protocol. The natural evolution from "just an investment vehicle" into an actual revenue generating company by tokenizing each individual robotics deal and giving people direct ownership, while taking a cut on the trading fees, all accruing to $DEUS. Essentially, imagine RoboStrategy and xStocks / PreStocks having a baby.

I know it feels wrong to buy things at a premium to NAV, but sometimes there is a bigger picture worth digging into.
tweets.full_text:
$DEUS is currently trading at a $17M mcap, $34M FDV. If you deduct the 30% supply being held and governed by the DAO, it's just a $24M FDV.

So the fairest metric would be to say that XMAQUINA is trading at a 2.4x to NAV.

In comparison, RoboStrategy / $BOT is trading at a 5.1x to NAV with a strong uptrend and narrative forming around it.

There is an interesting catch up trade here in my opinion, on top of some additional tailwinds:

1) I still think robotics is not a consensus trade right now, but the "ChatGPT moment" of physical AI is yet to come. There will be explosive demand for these very inaccessible companies, and $DEUS and $BOT are currently the only vehicles out there that allow you to get exposure to them.

2) What's even more interesting is that XMAQUINA will soon evolve into a Robotics Capital Markets protocol. The natural evolution from "just an investment vehicle" into an actual revenue generating company by tokenizing each individual robotics deal and giving people direct ownership, while taking a cut on the trading fees, all accruing to $DEUS. Essentially, imagine RoboStrategy and xStocks / PreStocks having a baby.

I know it feels wrong to buy things at a premium to NAV, but sometimes there is a bigger picture worth digging into.

thread_tweets.text: Due to the nature of private investments, NAV cannot be updated in real time and there can often be delays in changes in NAV. The usage of last round valuations for reporting NAV is also a subjective determination of fair value which may not reflect other factors that could influence someone’s determination of value.

Due to the nature of private investments, NAV cannot be updated in real time and there can often be delays in changes in NAV. The usage of last round valuations for reporting NAV is also a subjective determination of fair value which may not reflect other factors that could influence someone’s determination of value.

tweets.text:
Trump's AI EO has no heavy regulation, no permission frameworks, just a voluntary preview of models 30 days before release. Anthropic immediately filed a draft S-1 for an IPO, all while ChatGPT hit 1 billion users faster than any other product in human history. The US is telling the world: we compete, we don't constrain.

-- Claude: 56M monthly users, 640% YoY. ChatGPT: 1B monthly users, 62% YoY.

-- Microsoft dropped 7 in-house AI models at Build 2026, zero distillation from OpenAI. 

-- 1.8M teachers proposed banning screens through 2nd grade and taxing AI companies.

-- Bernie Sanders proposed 50% public ownership of all frontier labs.

-- 130 mathematicians signed the Leiden Declaration against AI weeks after AI solved an 80-year-old Erdős conjecture.

tweets.text: Don’t invent stories. The market had a strong run, then it had a correction. The explanations came afterward, as they always do. Most of the narratives people attach to market moves are hindsight rationalizations and rarely provide any alpha. Now we’re back to business, hopefully at a more moderate pace. A healthy market isn’t one that goes straight up forever.

tweets.text:
No one is exempt from this process. Having it go well depends on people's abilities to make frank assessments of strengths and weaknesses (most importantly weaknesses). While it's generally as difficult for managers to give this feedback as it is for their subordinates to hear it, in the long run it makes people happier and the organization more successful.
#principleoftheday

tweets.text:
Recent UK crypto sanctions seem to be a bit of an overreach. 

Wonder if it will ever get to the point where it’s ignored because HTX address tainting onchain has been catastrophic. 

In the past sanctions were done and those crypto businesses typically had a high % of illicit activity (Huione, Blender, Hydra, etc).

While Justin Sun may be a controversial name HTX has a decent number of Asia retail users.

Basically now I’ve had to ignore the sanctions category when tracing cases by exposure since “risk” itself has become meaningless. 

Also compliance tools are a bit flawed and do not differentiate pre & post sanctions activity well. 

Meanwhile I have a legit $1.25B laundering case by an illicit actor the UK completely failed to detect. 

Given UK incompetence historically in crypto cases it’s not surprising to see them sanction HTX and miss the actual violations…..
